title: A method for calculating entropy production without knowing transition rates or dwell times
abstract: The entropy production rate is central to the study of non-equilibrium systems. This parameter is closely connected to violation of time-reversal symmetry, energy consumption, efficiency, and other properties of interest; in short, it quantifies how far a system is from thermodynamic equilibrium. Standard formulas for the entropy production require knowledge of the system's underlying dynamics, but this knowledge may be hard to acquire in practice. Here, I present a method for inferring the entropy production rate of a Markovian system from the sequence of states that the system occupies on a single long trajectory or many shorter trajectories, and the total time of the trajectory or trajectories. The method does not require knowledge of the dwell times in the various states, the times at which transitions occur, or of the transition rates characterizing the Markov process.
summary: This paper introduces a method to calculate the entropy production rate of a Markovian system without needing to know transition rates or dwell times. By using only the sequence of states and total elapsed time, the method allows for the estimation of thermodynamic properties from incomplete trajectory data. It also demonstrates that choosing specific cycle bases can optimize the convergence of this estimator.
main_problem: Calculating the entropy production rate in non-equilibrium systems is difficult when underlying transition rates, dwell times, or transition times are unknown or unobservable.
main_result: The author presents a new method to infer the entropy production rate using only the sequence of visited states and the total trajectory time, bypassing the need for transition rates or dwell times.
method: The method uses a fundamental-cycle decomposition (spanning tree approach) to express entropy production in terms of the log-ratio of forward to reverse cycle occurrences.
model_or_system: The model consists of Markovian systems with discrete microstates and continuous-time dynamics, represented by a strongly connected, reversible directed graph governed by a master equation.
key_observables: Entropy production rate, net flux, cycle affinity, and the number of times a cycle occurs in a trajectory.
important_parameters: Total trajectory time, sequence of states, and the choice of cycle basis for convergence optimization.
main_assumptions: The underlying dynamics must be Markovian, the system must have a finite number of discrete states, and the graph must be strongly connected and reversible.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a biological active transport system; Figure 2 shows spanning trees and cycle decomposition via chords; Figure 3 compares full vs. coarse-grained systems; Figure 4 demonstrates the convergence of the estimator using different cycle bases.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of calculating entropy production, introduces the mathematical framework of Markov graphs and master equations, presents the new inference method based on cycle affinities, validates the method via Gillespie simulations, and discusses the impact of cycle basis selection and coarse-graining.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it provides a way to quantify non-equilibrium properties and dissipation in systems where the full microscopic dynamics are not experimentally resolvable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A biological system used to illustrate the central result of the paper. In this system, a protein complex E, which spans the cell membrane, facilitates active transport of a small molecule L against the chemical potential gradient. (A) is a schematic of the protein complex in conformation Ei, in which E can only interact with small molecules L and M inside the cell membrane at chemical potentials µM i and µL i . (B) Shows conformation Eo, in which the binding sites for L and M face the outside of the cell and binding of L and M take place at chemical potentials µM o and µL o . (C) shows a graphical representation of the possible states of E, also reflecting the limitation that L...

Figure 2: Top row: spanning trees of the graph in Fig. 1 Bottom row: the fundamental set of cycles associated with each spanning tree; the chords associated with each cycle are shown as dashed rather than solid lines.

Figure 3: Top: The system described in Fig. 1, together with a decomposition into fundamental cycles and a hypothetical sequence σX associated with a possible trajectory X. Bottom: a coarse graining of the same system corresponding to the binding state being unobservable if the protein complex is in conformation Ei. A decomposition of this coarse-grained system into fundamental cycles is also shown, along with the blurred sequence that would be recorded by an observer subject to this limitation.
Figure 4: Results of Gillespie simulations modeling the system described in the Illustrative Example. (A)
